---
title: "Analysis: Vendored Git Dependency Workflow in extendr/rextendr Packages"
output: html_document
---

## The Problem

When `closure-core` (a git dependency) gains new public API — for example `closure_count()` or `ResultListFromMeanSdN::empty()` — the Rust compiler in `unsum` reports errors like:

```
error[E0432]: unresolved import `closure_core::closure_count`
error[E0599]: no function or associated item named `empty` found for struct `ResultListFromMeanSdN`
```

These look like Rust API errors, but the *actual cause* is that `Cargo.lock` still pins `closure-core` to an old commit that predates those functions.

---

## How the Build System Works

`unsum` uses the standard rextendr vendoring setup:

1. **`src/rust/vendor.tar.xz`** — the source of truth. Contains a snapshot of all Rust dependencies.
2. **`src/rust/vendor-config.toml`** — tells cargo to resolve all sources (crates.io and git) from the extracted vendor directory.
3. **`src/Makevars.in`** — at build time, if `vendor.tar.xz` exists, it is extracted to `src/vendor/` and `vendor-config.toml` is placed at `src/.cargo/config.toml`. Then `cargo build --offline` is run.

```makefile
if [ -f ./rust/vendor.tar.xz ]; then \
    tar xf rust/vendor.tar.xz && \
    mkdir -p $(CARGOTMP) && \
    cp rust/vendor-config.toml $(CARGOTMP)/config.toml; \
fi
```

This means **`vendor.tar.xz` is used for every build** (local `devtools::install()` and CRAN), not just CRAN. The expanded `src/rust/vendor/` directory you see in the repo is just the remnant of a previous build; it is not what gets compiled.

The `vendor-config.toml` redirects the git source:

```toml
[source."git+https://github.com/lhdjung/closure-core.git"]
git = "https://github.com/lhdjung/closure-core.git"
replace-with = "vendored-sources"

[source.vendored-sources]
directory = "vendor"
```

So cargo uses the vendored closure-core files regardless of what commit hash is in `Cargo.lock` — **but the vendored files are only as new as the last time `vendor.tar.xz` was created**.

---

## The Root Cause

`Cargo.lock` pins git dependencies to a specific commit:

```toml
[[package]]
name = "closure-core"
version = "1.0.0"
source = "git+https://github.com/lhdjung/closure-core.git#d1aaf214e4fcba8b4891f97e66fedf8c9babf34d"
```

When new API is added to `closure-core` and pushed to GitHub, two things must happen for `unsum` to see it:

1. **`Cargo.lock` must be updated** to reference the new commit hash.
2. **`vendor.tar.xz` must be regenerated** to contain the new source files.

If only (1) happens (e.g., by manually editing `Cargo.lock`), the Cargo.lock now points to a newer commit, but the vendored source files inside `vendor.tar.xz` are still the old ones. Cargo is redirected to the vendor directory, which doesn't have the new functions — hence the compilation error.

If only (2) happens without updating `Cargo.lock`, cargo may refuse to build because the lock file does not match.

Both must be done together, in the right order.

---

## The Recurring Manual Fix (and Why It Works Anyway)

Manually editing the git hash in `Cargo.lock` (line 363 at the time of writing) *sometimes* works, because:

- The vendor config matches by base git URL, not by commit hash. So any hash pointing to `github.com/lhdjung/closure-core.git` will be redirected to the vendored directory.
- If the vendored closure-core happens to already have the new API (e.g., because `vendor.tar.xz` was recently regenerated), updating the hash alone is sufficient.
- If the vendored closure-core does *not* have the new API, the hash update does nothing and the build still fails.

In the incident that prompted this document: `vendor.tar.xz` contained the old closure-core at `d1aaf214`, which lacked both `closure_count` and `ResultListFromMeanSdN::empty()`. Updating the hash in `Cargo.lock` to the newer commit `da4ef4c4` worked because — presumably — `vendor.tar.xz` had already been regenerated at some point to include the new API. The hash update just corrected the mismatch between what the lock file claimed and what was vendored.

This is fragile. It relies on implicit knowledge of what's actually inside `vendor.tar.xz`.

---

## The Correct Workflow

The right tool for regenerating the vendor is `rextendr::vendor_pkgs()`. It runs `cargo vendor`, collects all dependencies (including git ones) into `src/rust/vendor/`, and archives them as `vendor.tar.xz`.

The problem with git dependencies is that `cargo vendor` uses whatever commit is pinned in `Cargo.lock`. To pick up new commits, the lock file must be updated *first*.

**Full correct workflow after updating `closure-core` on GitHub:**

```r
# From the unsum project root in R:

# Step 1: Update Cargo.lock to the latest closure-core commit.
# This requires temporarily bypassing the vendor redirect so cargo can
# reach the real git source. Do this in a terminal:
#
#   cd src/rust
#   mv ../../.cargo/config.toml /tmp/backup-vendor-config.toml  # or just delete src/.cargo/
#   cargo update -p closure-core
#   mv /tmp/backup-vendor-config.toml ../../.cargo/config.toml
#
# OR: just manually replace the commit hash in Cargo.lock (line with "closure-core").

# Step 2: Re-vendor with the updated Cargo.lock.
rextendr::vendor_pkgs()
# This runs cargo vendor, creates vendor.tar.xz, and removes the expanded vendor/ dir.
```

`rextendr::vendor_pkgs()` handles step 2 correctly. The awkward part is step 1.

### Easier Step 1: Manual Hash Update

Since closure-core is a repo you control, the pragmatic approach for step 1 is:

1. Find the latest commit hash on GitHub (or via `git -C /path/to/closure-core rev-parse HEAD`).
2. Replace the old hash in `src/rust/Cargo.lock` at the `closure-core` entry.
3. Then run `rextendr::vendor_pkgs()`.

```bash
# Get the latest commit hash from the local clone:
git -C ~/rust_projects/closure-core rev-parse HEAD
```

Then in `src/rust/Cargo.lock`, find:
```
source = "git+https://github.com/lhdjung/closure-core.git#<OLD_HASH>"
```
and replace `<OLD_HASH>` with the new one.

Then in R: `rextendr::vendor_pkgs()`.

---

## Why the Diagnosis Is Confusing

Several things make this hard to debug:

1. **The error looks like a Rust API mismatch**, not a version/vendoring problem. "No `closure_count` in the root" sounds like the function was removed or renamed, not that an old snapshot is being compiled.

2. **Cargo reports the git source in compile output** even when the vendor redirect is active:
   ```
   Compiling closure-core v1.0.0 (https://github.com/lhdjung/closure-core.git#d1aaf214)
   ```
   This makes it look like cargo is fetching from GitHub and the new code *should* be there.

3. **The expanded `src/rust/vendor/` directory** (remnant of previous builds) may contain newer files than `vendor.tar.xz`, creating a false impression of what's actually compiled. The tarball always wins.

4. **`vendor.tar.xz` is present during local development**, so the offline path is always taken — it's not only for CRAN. Developers may not realize their local builds are also using the tarball.

---

## Potential Improvement to rextendr

`rextendr::vendor_pkgs()` could optionally run `cargo update -p <crate>` (or `cargo update` broadly) before vendoring. A possible interface:

```r
# Hypothetical future API:
rextendr::vendor_pkgs(update = "closure-core")
# or
rextendr::vendor_pkgs(update = TRUE)  # update all
```

This would:
1. Temporarily bypass the vendor redirect (modify or remove the CARGO_HOME config).
2. Run `cargo update -p closure-core` to fetch the latest commit from git.
3. Run `cargo vendor` as usual.
4. Restore any config changes.
5. Archive as `vendor.tar.xz`.

This would make the whole "update a git dependency" workflow a single R function call, which is the appropriate level of abstraction for this tooling.

The relevant rextendr source is in `R/cran-compliance.R`. The `vendor_pkgs()` function is the right place to add this capability.

---

## Summary

| What changed | What you must do |
|---|---|
| New crate added to `Cargo.toml` | Update `Cargo.lock` hash (or `cargo update`), then `vendor_pkgs()` |
| Existing git dep gets new API | Update `Cargo.lock` hash (or `cargo update`), then `vendor_pkgs()` |
| Existing crates.io dep bumped in `Cargo.toml` | `vendor_pkgs()` (cargo resolves the new version automatically) |
| No dependency changes | Nothing needed |

**The two-step rule:** update `Cargo.lock` first, then regenerate `vendor.tar.xz` via `rextendr::vendor_pkgs()`.

---

## References

- [rextendr on CRAN](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/rextendr/rextendr.pdf)
- [rextendr source: R/cran-compliance.R](https://rdrr.io/github/extendr/rextendr/src/R/cran-compliance.R)
- [Using Rust code in R packages (rextendr vignette)](https://extendr.rs/rextendr/articles/package.html)
- [rextendr GitHub](https://github.com/extendr/rextendr)
